summary.intent: Migrate the web-ui JavaScript codebase (~40K LoC) from plain JS with HTM tagged templates to TypeScript with JSX/TSX, using Vite as the build tool and Preact as the UI framework.
summary.outcome: The migration was substantially completed across ~130 files, converting all stores, utilities, locale files, components, pages, and entry points to TypeScript/TSX, with Vite build tooling set up. The session hit rate limits twice before finishing, and the final state of large pages (ChatPage, SettingsPage, ChannelsPage) and Rust integration changes were still in progress at session end.

repo: The project is a Rust/Axum web app (moltis) where JS assets are embedded via `include_dir!` in crates/web/src/assets.rs — adding a build step requires updating the Rust asset pipeline to point at Vite output.
The UI uses HTM tagged templates (`html\`...\``) with Preact signals for reactivity, served as native ES modules with zero build tooling prior to this migration.
Locale files are split into 54 files across en/fr/zh namespaces, each exporting typed translation objects — a purely mechanical conversion to TS.
The app has two separate entry points: main.tsx (main SPA) and login-app.tsx / onboarding-app.tsx (standalone flows), all needing separate Vite entry configuration.
Stores use Preact signals (@preact/signals) for shared reactive state; these were identified as the highest-ROI TS conversion targets due to clean separation from HTM templates.
The codebase uses a `gon` object (server-injected globals) that needed a typed interface in types/gon.ts to avoid `any` proliferation.
JSDoc annotations were already present in channel-utils.js, helpers.js, and other utility files, providing a migration head start.

code.finding: New Vite config created to handle multi-entry SPA build, replacing zero-build-step native ES module serving.
New tsconfig created with Preact JSX transform settings (jsxImportSource: preact).
Central type barrel file created aggregating channel, gon, model, rpc, and session type definitions.
Typed interface for server-injected `window.gon` globals, critical for eliminating implicit `any` across the codebase.

workflow: Parallelizing subagent work across codebase layers (types, stores, utilities, pages) significantly accelerates large mechanical migrations.
Rate limits on Claude Code sessions mid-migration require checkpointing: checking which files were already converted before resuming is essential to avoid duplication.
For HTM→JSX migrations, the template literal syntax swap is purely mechanical but volume-intensive; automated subagent batching by file size or layer is effective.
The recommendation was Option A (JSDoc+checkJs, zero migration) but the user chose Option C (full migration); documenting the tradeoffs in the plan file before starting was the right call.

friction: Hit Claude Code rate limits twice during the session (at ~7pm and 12am Europe/Lisbon), interrupting parallel subagent batches mid-execution.
After each rate limit reset, had to re-survey which files were already converted vs still pending, adding overhead.
The largest pages (page-settings.js at 5656 LoC, onboarding-view.js at 4523 LoC) were deferred to the final batch, meaning rate limits hit just as the heaviest work began.

The zero-build-step architecture meant Rust asset embedding (assets.rs, templates.rs, spa.rs) needs non-trivial changes to point at Vite output — this integration work was identified but not yet completed.
open_items: Update Rust asset pipeline (crates/web/src/assets.rs, templates.rs, spa.rs) to serve Vite build output instead of raw JS files from include_dir!.
Verify ChatPage.tsx, SettingsPage.tsx, and ChannelsPage.tsx (the three largest pages) were fully converted and compile cleanly.
Run `tsc --noEmit` and `vite build` to surface any remaining type errors or import resolution issues across the full ~130-file migration.
Delete or archive the original JS source files once the TS/TSX versions are confirmed working, to avoid dual-maintenance confusion.
Update CI/CD pipeline to run `npm run build` before the Rust `cargo build` step.
Consider adding E2E test updates (Playwright, ~11K LoC) to cover the new build output paths — currently deferred as low value.
The service worker (sw.ts) may need Vite-specific handling (vite-plugin-pwa or manual registration) since it was a standalone file previously.
Validate that the Preact signals store reactivity still works correctly after the TS conversion, particularly computed selectors.
